ground_truth.title: 24 Types of Restaurants: Complete Guide for Potential Owners
ground_truth.author: Sling Team
ground_truth.main_content:
If your dream is to be an owner or manager of a food-service business, understanding the options available to you — the types of restaurants you can open — is an essential first step in your success. The restaurant industry bases its classification system on a number of variables, including menu style, preparation method, pricing, seating options, and means by which the food is served to the customer. This guide introduces the most common types of restaurants.

Common types include: Fine dining restaurants offer diners an upscale meal experience often comprising several courses, creating a stylish atmosphere of elegance and exclusivity. Casual dining features table service, moderately priced food, and low-key atmosphere with unique decor. Fast casual offers quality food with counter service, healthier than fast food and more affordable than casual dining. Ghost restaurants serve customers exclusively through online or phone orders without a traditional storefront. Family style restaurants have servers deliver food in large dishes for self-service. Fast food focuses on price, convenience, and speed with counter or drive-thru service. Food trucks, carts, or stands specialize in a single type of food with limited menus. Cafes feature outdoor seating, coffee, tea, pastries, and an unhurried atmosphere. Buffets allow customers to serve themselves from a selection of food at a fixed price. Pubs offer beer and expanded menus in a festive atmosphere with table and bar service.

Other types include: Cafeterias serve ready-cooked food from behind a counter. Coffee houses focus on hot beverages with limited cold food options. Diners are characterized by fried foods, burgers, and breakfast items at low cost. Pop-up restaurants allow owners to experiment with different concepts temporarily. Contemporary casual restaurants cater to young professionals with modern, trendy concepts like farm-to-table and locally sourced ingredients. Bistros serve hearty meals at moderate prices in a not-so-formal setting. Destination restaurants draw patrons from beyond their immediate area. Teppanyaki grills specialize in Japanese cuisine prepared dramatically in front of patrons. Mongolian barbecues let diners assemble bowls of raw ingredients that are stir-fried on a griddle. Concession stands are found in ballparks, theaters, and festivals. Digital-only restaurants have no seating, only a counter for pickup. Theme restaurants use specific themes like medieval, pirate, or sports. Bakeries focus on quality baked goods. Delivery-only restaurants operate without public access, preparing food for delivery only.

Effective scheduling is essential for all types of restaurants. Sling offers powerful scheduling tools including time and attendance tracking, labor cost optimization, messaging features, and task management to help streamline operations.
with: Fine dining restaurants offer diners an upscale meal experience often comprising several courses (e.g., salad, appetizer, entree, dessert).
Fast casual restaurants cater to customers who are looking for fare that is relatively quick yet, at the same time, healthier than fast food and more affordable than a casual dining establishment.

A ghost restaurant (a.k.a. virtual restaurant, delivery-only restaurant, online-only restaurant, or dark kitchen) is a food-service business that serves customers exclusively through phone orders, online orders, or both.

Food trucks, carts, or stands are unique modern businesses that normally specialize in a single type of food (e.g., tacos, sandwiches, hot dogs, ice cream, smoothies, etc.) and serve a limited menu that revolves around those items.

Effective scheduling is the key to that organization. Restaurants of all kinds — from traditional brick-and-mortar establishments to virtual restaurants and everything in-between — are discovering the simplicity and value of all-in-one workforce management software like Sling.
